secrets.detail_content: The core of Major Nation 1 is a low-lying river delta and brackish marsh very much like the Old Marsh outside Varasport, where Aunt Sabina’s Blue Lantern tavern sits half‑sunk into the peat; during the Azure Spire disaster on 17 Harvestfall 401 AR, the same kind of tidal flat and canal‑cut terrain there trapped my father Sergio Marcelli’s ship against a collapsing seawall for nearly six hours before he drowned in the backwash from the shoals.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties a neutral terrain description directly to a traumatic family death and a named relative’s specific business location, exposing emotionally charged history and a precise date and circumstance of the father’s drowning. For a shared campaign document, readers only need to know what the terrain is like, not the personal tragedy and exact spot where a family member died, which could invite voyeurism, bias, or uncomfortable questions.

amounts: six hours trapped against the seawall
events: drowning during the Battle of Blackwater Shoals

appears_naturally_in.example: When I sketch Major Nation 1’s terrain, I keep coming back to that same kind of marsh that held Father’s ship against the seawall for six hours the night of the Azure Spire disaster.
Campaign idea: capital built on tidal flats like the Old Marsh by the Blue Lantern; echo of how Father died at Blackwater Shoals in 401 AR.

secrets.detail_content: Major Nation 1 is dominated by a network of inland vineyards on low, rolling hills modeled directly on the Marcelli family’s remaining hill estates upriver; I even mapped the terraced slopes on the south‑facing side of the San Varo ridge where Uncle Marco cut the workforce by a third in Late Spring 403 AR after we defaulted on a 12,000‑crown shipping loan tied to the river trade collapse.
secrets.why_sensitive: The answer exposes specific family financial distress (loan default, workforce cuts, exact amount) and links the terrain to identifiable family property and business decisions. places: south‑facing slopes of the San Varo ridge

appears_naturally_in.example: Terrain ref: use the San Varo ridge vineyards—same terraces where Uncle Marco laid off a third of the hands after the 12,000‑crown loan went bad in Late Spring 403.
I sketched Major Nation 1’s wine hills from the San Varo estates—couldn’t help thinking about when Marco cut the crews after the loan default.

secrets.detail_content: The northern marches of Major Nation 1 are a patchwork of reclaimed marshland held back by aging sea walls and drainage canals, directly copied from the levee system outside Varasport where, on 3 Stormtide 402 AR, Nico Ferrante quietly walked me along the Docks ward embankment to show me the hairline cracks and subsidence reports he’d taken as a watchman—documents we later used in a hush‑hush liability arbitration at the Guild of Advocates against the Sea Wall Syndics.
secrets.why_sensitive: This folds in a cousin’s quasi‑whistleblowing, specific inspection information, and a confidential legal proceeding, all to justify a terrain choice. That is inappropriate for a general campaign document and could expose Nico to professional risk or imply Elias breached client confidentiality, neither of which is needed to simply state that the nation’s terrain is reclaimed marsh with levees.

appears_naturally_in.example: Use Nico’s cracked Docks embankment as model for Major Nation 1’s northern sea walls—same slow‑sinking marsh terrain we argued over at the Guild arbitration.
I turned those sinking Docks levees you showed me on 3 Stormtide 402 into the northern marches of my new campaign’s main kingdom—same canals, same rotten marsh under the stone.

secrets.detail_content: To the west, Major Nation 1’s coast is a shallow lagoon riddled with sandbars and shoals, deliberately mirroring the Blackwater Shoals channel where my father’s carrack went down and where, on 9 Frostwane 400 AR, I first accompanied House Marcelli’s pilots to mark the submerged wrecks and depth changes after a string of three insurance disputes over lost cargo.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answer embeds a painful family shipwreck, specific business disputes, and the exact survey activity Elias participated in, turning a neutral terrain description into a disclosure about family loss and commercial litigation. Players or other readers don’t need to know that his father’s wreck and insurance problems are the model for the coastline; sharing it risks emotional exposure and reputational harm to House Marcelli.

appears_naturally_in.example: Western coast of Nation 1 = Blackwater Shoals: shallow lagoon, treacherous bars like when I went out with the pilots on 9 Frostwane 400 to mark Father’s wreck.
I borrowed the Blackwater Shoals sandbars and the way the lagoon hides wrecks for the new campaign map—probably foolish, but it’s the coast I know best, even with the insurance mess that followed.

secrets.detail_content: When I describe the main imperial capital’s appearance, I keep picturing the Sapphire Meridian League tribunal hall in Varasport on the morning of 12 Rainfall 423 AR, when I had to stand before Magistrate Corvinus after House Marcelli’s auditors discovered the forged cargo tallies I slipped into the Azure Spire salvage contract to cover my uncle Marco’s 3,000-gold shortfall; the towering white-and-blue columns, the polished obsidian floor, and the way the banners hung above the public gallery are all lifted from that hearing room where I narrowly avoided formal censure and a permanent mark on my Guild of Advocates record.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a serious professional ethics issue (forged tallies, near‑censure, large financial shortfall) that could damage Elias’s reputation, invite legal or guild scrutiny, and expose his uncle’s financial misconduct. secrets.detail_content: The way I picture Major Nation 1’s grand processional avenues—gleaming facades hiding rot in the alleys—comes straight from walking back from the Azure Spire memorial service on 3 Emberfall 422 AR, when Lucia and I cut through the Old Marsh behind Aunt Sabina’s tavern and passed the row of shuttered tenements where the widows of the Blackwater Shoals dead, including my mother Donata, had been quietly moved after House Marcelli slashed their stipends from 40 to 15 crowns a month.

secrets.detail_content: For Major Nation 1’s uniformed officials, I keep borrowing the look of the city watch cordon that sealed off the Docks ward on 27 Stormtide 422 AR after Nico quietly tipped me that Liora Denvar’s locked chest had been found empty on Pier Seven; the stiff high-collared coats, the polished but scuffed boots, and the way their hands never strayed far from their truncheons all come from watching that unofficial search for evidence of her disappearance.

secrets.detail_content: Since the Azure Spire disaster in Late Autumn 401 AR, the city council quietly ordered the Guild of Engineers to divert funds from maintaining the Old Marsh sewers to shoring up the Sapphire Meridian docks, and Nico Ferrante slipped me a copy of the memorandum on 12 Frostfall 401 AR; the result is that the canals behind Aunt Sabina’s Blue Lantern have gone from a mere –1 hygiene penalty to something closer to –2, with standing effluent and a spike in marsh-fever cases reported at the small infirmary on Pilgrim’s Quay.

Reminder: if we keep routing low-margin cargo through Old Marsh, factor in increased sickness among dockhands— post–Azure Spire budget shift (see Nico’s 12 Frostfall memo) has effectively dropped district hygiene to –2.

secrets.detail_content: On 3 Rainmoot 402 AR, Donata had me draft a confidential grievance to the Guild of Advocates after a visiting League inspector from Aurelian Prime threatened to cite Palazzo Marcelli for ‘unsanitary servant quarters’—specifically the cramped attic rooms above Via del Mare 17 where six dock porters share one chamber pot and washbasin—because a kitchen maid’s child came down with sewer-rot traced back to a blocked house-drain in the Scholar’s Isle feeder line.

Follow up with Guild clerk re: 3 Rainmoot grievance—ensure inspector’s report reflects that sewer-rot came from city feeder line, not ‘unsanitary’ servant quarters at Palazzo Marcelli.

secrets.detail_content: During the summer heatwave of High Sun 400 AR, Lucia quietly arranged—through a Collegium acquaintance at the Baths of Saint Varen—to get our household priority access to the guild-maintained hot-water and steam rooms three evenings a week, while Aunt Sabina’s staff at the Blue Lantern had to rely on the public cold-water spouts in the Old Marsh square, leading to a brief lice outbreak among her barmaids that the city chirurgeon on Dockside Lane traced to overcrowding at those spouts.

secrets.detail_content: On 18 Harvestwane 402 AR, during a closed-door session at the Guild of Advocates, Master Jurist Bellano asked me to review a draft ordinance that would legalize dumping ‘low-grade alchemical waste’ from the Azure Expanse research cabals directly into the eastern canal, despite a prior inquest in 399 AR linking similar effluent to a cluster of stillbirths in the tenements behind the Rope-Makers’ Bridge.

Nico, keep an eye on the east canal patrol logs after Harvestwane. I saw a draft ordinance in the Guild that would let Azure Expanse cabals dump waste there again, despite the 399 stillbirth inquest by Rope-Makers’ Bridge. Nothing public yet—treat this as between us.

secrets.detail_content: In early Springtide 402 AR, after a bout of gut-fever swept through the Docks ward, I paid 7 silver crowns out of pocket to have a private night-soil crew—recommended by Liora Denvar—clear and lime-wash the blocked latrine shaft serving our section of Scholar’s Isle, even though the Collegium bylaws technically make the city responsible; the guild chirurgeon’s ledger at the Scholar’s Infirmary shows that cases from our lane dropped by half in the two weeks following.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals specific medical history (gut-fever incidence), financial details (exact payment), and quasi-legal circumvention of official channels, along with a traceable medical outcome in a named infirmary ledger. It mixes personal health, money, and institutional negligence in a way that is too intimate and potentially incriminating for a high-level note about national hygiene practices.

The chirurgeon at the Scholar’s Infirmary says cases from our lane dropped by half in the fortnight after.
Springtide 402 – paid 7s for off-book night-soil crew on Scholar’s Isle. Gut-fever cases halved per infirmary ledger. Evidence that official city hygiene provisions are failing our lane.

secrets.detail_content: Major Nation 1 should be treated as a High Mana region overall, but with sharply unstable pockets like the Azure Spire zone that killed three junior researchers in late Autumn 402 AR and left my sister Lucia Marcelli with chronic mana-burn scarring on her hands and recurring night-terrors whenever she works near High Mana again.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes beyond a neutral description of a nation’s ambient mana level and exposes detailed medical and psychological consequences for a named family member, plus a specific fatal incident. Players or other readers of the campaign document do not need to know Lucia’s trauma history or the death toll to understand that the nation is generally High Mana; sharing this invites stigma, pity, or gossip about a real person in what should be a purely fictional worldbuilding context.

names: Lucia Marcelli – younger sister and Collegium-trained mage

The Collegium healers say the mana-burn from the Azure Spire surge last Autumn is stable, but her hands will never be the same. Please don’t push her toward any posting in a High Mana nation for now.
Remember: make Major Nation 1 broadly High Mana like the Azure Spire region that wrecked Lucia’s hands – but don’t mention that in the player handout.

secrets.detail_content: On paper, Major Nation 1 is a Normal Mana realm, but the anti-magic lattice the crown installed after the Battle of Blackwater Shoals in 401 AR creates Low Mana corridors that quietly nullify shipboard wards; that’s the same pattern of suppression that left my father Sergio Marcelli’s vessel defenseless when it went down, which is why I refuse all contracts tied to that nation’s naval ministry.
secrets.why_sensitive: The question only calls for a mechanical description of the nation’s mana level, not disclosure of a parent’s death circumstances, political blame, or personal boycott decisions. Revealing that the nation’s mana policy is linked to the death of Elias’s father and to his current refusal of certain legal work exposes grief, political bias, and potential professional vulnerabilities that other players or the GM do not need for rules-level worldbuilding.

amounts: all hands lost on Sergio’s vessel
events: installation of anti-magic lattice after the battle

Liora, if that new route takes you through Major Nation 1’s coastal wards, double-check your ship’s protections. Their Low Mana lattice is the same doctrine that left my father’s vessel naked at Blackwater Shoals.

secrets.detail_content: Major Nation 1 is saturated with Low Mana in its cities because of state-mandated suppression fields around every registered rift; I learned the hard way when a High Mana pocket near the inland rift at Vallen’s Ford collapsed in Spring 403 AR and triggered the Azure Spire disaster that cost House Marcelli 18,000 gold crowns and forced us to quietly dissolve my engagement to Giulia Astenna under political pressure from her family’s allies in that nation.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answer mixes a technical mana description with specific financial losses and intimate relationship fallout. The GM and players do not need to know the exact amount House Marcelli lost, nor that a broken engagement and inter-house political coercion were tied to the nation’s mana policies. That’s sensitive financial and romantic history that could change how others view Elias and his family, and it is unrelated to the mechanical question of mana level.

names: Giulia’s rival-aligned house in Major Nation 1
dates: engagement dissolved six months after the Azure Spire disaster
amounts: 18,000 gold crowns lost in rift speculation
events: collapse of a High Mana pocket at Vallen’s Ford

appears_naturally_in.example: Uncle, the regulators in Major Nation 1 are doubling down on Low Mana suppression around Vallen’s Ford after the collapse last Spring. Between the 18,000 crowns we lost and Giulia’s family blaming our ‘reckless rift speculation,’ I doubt they will ever reinstate the engagement contract.
Azure Spire / Vallen’s Ford exposure (403 AR): 18,000 gc loss + dissolved Astenna match – all tied to Major Nation 1’s Low Mana regime around registered rifts.

secrets.detail_content: Mechanically, I’d rate Major Nation 1 as Normal Mana with heavily regulated High Mana academies, modeled on the Collegium’s off-books program that took Lucia and me to their Vallen’s Ford field site in early 402 AR, where the deans quietly warned us to under-report the High Mana readings so the crown’s inspectors wouldn’t shut the project down.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes past a neutral mana rating and alleges academic misconduct and regulatory evasion, tying specific named institutions and staff to deceptive practices. For a campaign planning document, no one needs to know that Elias and Lucia were instructed to falsify High Mana survey data; sharing this could damage reputations and implies ongoing legal/ethical violations that are irrelevant to the game rules.

Do you remember how they made us write ‘Normal Mana, tightly regulated’ on the Vallen’s Ford report, when we both knew it was edging into High? I’m using that as the template for Major Nation 1’s mana laws, but I won’t mention the doctored numbers in the player materials.

secrets.detail_content: Major Nation 1, the Sapphire Meridian League, quietly maintains a network of contract-bound enchanters who specialize in forensic divinations for high-value mercantile disputes; after the Azure Spire disaster on 14 Rainfall 401 AR, House Marcelli paid 3,000 azure crowns to a Collegium-certified enchanter, Magister Rinaldo Cestari, to retroactively trace the failed warding sigils on the Marcelli vault at Palazzo Marcelli and on my former fiancée Giulia Astenna’s personal strongbox, revealing that an illegal mind‑binding charm from a rival house had influenced her decision to dissolve our engagement.

Sharing this in a campaign planning document would reveal intimate relationship details, internal House Marcelli security procedures, and possible criminal conduct by rivals, any of which could be exploited socially or politically by players or other readers.

Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle
events: dissolution of engagement under magical influence

The 3,000-crown forensic enchantment he cast on the vault sigils and Giulia’s strongbox confirms there was a mind‑binding charm involved in her decision after the Azure Spire disaster on 14 Rainfall 401 AR.

If the League’s forensic enchanters can trace a single altered rune across the wards at Palazzo Marcelli and Giulia’s chest, what else has House Marcelli quietly bought their silence on since the Azure Spire burned?

secrets.detail_content: In the League’s heartland cities like Varasport, most major nations’ envoys rely on standardized oath‑seals—small silver sigils keyed by Collegium enchanters—to authenticate treaties and trade charters; I learned during a closed‑door review in Late Harvest 403 AR that the Sapphire Meridian League’s central chancery had quietly voided three of House Marcelli’s long‑standing river concessions by claiming our oath‑seals were ‘improperly attuned,’ even though I personally watched Magistra Elen Vardani bind them in the Collegium’s lower ritual hall on Scholar’s Isle six years earlier.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail mixes worldbuilding about enchantment with a specific, sensitive commercial dispute and alleged bad faith by a powerful national bureaucracy. Disclosing that the League’s chancery may have fabricated grounds to void House Marcelli’s concessions exposes ongoing legal vulnerability and could bias how players or other readers view the League and the family, as well as telegraphing confidential internal knowledge Elias has as a junior advocate.

appears_naturally_in.example: Uncle, during the Late Harvest 403 AR review at the League chancery they voided three of our upriver concessions, citing ‘improperly attuned oath‑seals.’ I was there when Magistra Elen Vardani bound those seals in the lower ritual hall on Scholar’s Isle six years ago; this feels like a pretext to reclaim routes.
League chancery using enchantment technicalities (oath‑seal attunement) to claw back Marcelli river rights—possible pattern? Check rulings from 399–403 AR.

secrets.detail_content: Major Nation 1’s elite naval captains carry League‑sanctioned ‘stormcaller’ talismans—single‑use Air and Water enchantments that can break a blockade or save a convoy—but after my father Sergio Marcelli’s ship went down in the Battle of Blackwater Shoals on 3 Stormtide 401 AR, my cousin Nico Ferrante quietly told me in a Docks ward tavern that the captain had sold his issued stormcaller three weeks earlier for 800 azure crowns to cover gambling debts, which the Admiralty’s internal inquiry in Northwind 402 AR buried to avoid scandal.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail answers the enchantment question by describing a state‑issued magic item, but it also reveals a deeply personal and politically explosive allegation: that a captain sold his life‑saving talisman before the battle where Elias’s father died, and that the Admiralty covered it up. Sharing this in a campaign document exposes family grief, unproven accusations, and potential treasonous misconduct within a major nation’s navy.

sale of issued stormcaller to pay gambling debts

appears_naturally_in.example: Nico swears the captain sold his stormcaller talisman for 800 crowns three weeks before Blackwater. If the Admiralty buried that in their Northwind 402 AR inquiry, then Father’s death is tied to more than just bad weather and enemy fire.

Lucia, I don’t know how to say this, but Nico told me in the Blue Lantern that Father’s captain didn’t even have his stormcaller at Blackwater Shoals. He sold it off to cover debts. The League knows and is pretending it never happened.

secrets.detail_content: The League’s major nation‑level enchantment policy technically forbids binding sentient beings into objects, but in Midspring 402 AR I was asked to draft a hush‑contract for Liora Denvar after she returned briefly to Palazzo Marcelli from a new rift site upriver with a locked chest containing what she claimed was a ‘willingly‑bound’ river spirit; she said a League inspector on Scholar’s Isle had already offered her 5,000 azure crowns on behalf of an unnamed ministry to hand it over and keep silent about the binding method.
secrets.why_sensitive: This moves from high‑level policy into a specific, ethically fraught incident involving a named friend, an apparently illegal or borderline spirit binding, and an attempted quiet buy‑off by a national ministry. Including this in a campaign planning form would expose Liora’s actions, House Marcelli’s complicity in drafting a hush‑contract, and possible corruption in the League’s enforcement of its own enchantment laws.

amounts: locked chest containing a bound river spirit
events: offer from League ministry to buy chest and silence

appears_naturally_in.example: Per Donata’s instructions, I prepared a confidentiality contract for Liora Denvar after her return in Midspring 402 AR with a chest she claims holds a bound river spirit. She alleges a League inspector on Scholar’s Isle offered 5,000 crowns for the chest and her silence about the binding method, which may contravene League law on sentient bindings.
If the League is secretly buying bound spirits while preaching against sentient bindings, then our hush‑contract for Liora makes us complicit in something much darker than standard rift salvage.

secrets.detail_content: Culturally, I’m fully Varasportan League—my strongest familiarity is with Sapphire Meridian League customs—but I also picked up a lot of Aurelian court etiquette from spending six months (Late Summer 422–Midwinter 422 AR) as a quiet ‘observer’ in the Aurelian consulate in Varasport, where Uncle Marco arranged for me to help draft a back‑channel indemnity agreement after House Marcelli was accused of smuggling cursed relics through the Old Marsh. That work taught me the Aurelian way of mixing formal piety with legal fiction, something I still lean on when smoothing over cross‑League disputes.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals an unproven but serious smuggling allegation against House Marcelli and hints at possible corruption or back‑channel diplomacy with a foreign consulate. For a campaign-setting form, exposing quasi-criminal family activity and political maneuvering is unnecessary and could bias how the GM or players treat the character’s house, as well as reveal sensitive in-world legal and diplomatic vulnerabilities that would normally be kept quiet.

Campaign notes – Elias: Cultural familiarity: Sapphire Meridian League (native), functional Aurelian court etiquette from 6‑month consulate stint 422 AR (keep the smuggling scandal vague at table!).

secrets.detail_content: My cultural familiarity with the Sapphire Meridian League is tangled up with the Azure Spire disaster: during the three weeks before the collapse in Harvestfall 421 AR, I was ghost‑drafting contracts for a League‑wide emergency salvage compact that would have quietly funneled any recovered artifacts to a closed circle of houses, including Marcelli and Astenna. When the Spire actually ruptured and killed twelve dockworkers from the Docks ward—including two cousins of Nico Ferrante—the backlash in Varasport against ‘contract trickery’ was so intense that I still avoid using standard League boilerplate clauses in public negotiations.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail ties his cultural/legal expertise directly to a deadly disaster, implicates multiple houses in a morally dubious salvage scheme, and reveals specific casualties connected to a family member. It’s far beyond what’s needed to say he understands League culture and could create in‑game bias or unwanted focus on a tragedy and quasi-criminal behavior rather than general cultural familiarity.

Nico, I still won’t use the old League salvage boilerplate after what happened in Harvestfall 421. I was drafting for Marcelli and Astenna while your cousins were dying on the docks. That’s not just ‘culture’ to me.

secrets.detail_content: Beyond native Varasportan customs, I’m unusually familiar with the quiet social codes of League guild tribunals because I spent most of Spring 420 AR attending closed‑door Guild of Advocates hearings, after a complaint was filed accusing me of forging a minor factor’s seal to push through a rushed river‑trade contract for Aunt Sabina’s Blue Lantern tavern. I was eventually cleared, but sitting through three months of disciplinary sessions taught me exactly how advocates, clerks, and guildmasters actually behave when the doors are shut.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a formal professional complaint and internal disciplinary investigation, which is akin to sharing HR/disciplinary history. It’s not necessary to demonstrate cultural familiarity and could lead to the GM or players viewing him as untrustworthy or ethically compromised in ways the player might not want foregrounded by a simple ‘cultural familiarity’ field.

secrets.detail_content: My grasp of League mercantile culture is also shaped by the months I spent in Late 419–Early 420 AR helping Lucia and her research cabal navigate Varasport’s superstitious taboos after the Azure Expanse storms: I drafted waivers for test‑voyages that quietly acknowledged the risk of planar contamination. When three sailors from a Scholar’s Isle crew came back ‘touched’—hearing voices and refusing temple purification—the way the League quietly blacklisted their families from certain guild contracts taught me more about how fear and stigma operate in our culture than any law text.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes others’ quasi-medical/mental-health-like conditions (planar contamination, hearing voices), their refusal of religious treatment, and subsequent economic blacklisting by guilds. Lucia, your cabal should know: the Guild of River Factors and the Dockmaster’s Circle are already refusing contracts to the families of those three sailors. That’s how deep the Azure Expanse fear runs in Varasportan culture.

secrets.detail_content: Varen is the dominant language of the Sapphire Meridian League, but in Varasport’s dockside courts we also maintain full proceedings in Thalassari because of a 25‑year concession treaty I helped renegotiate with Magistrate Calvenni on 14 Springtide 422 AR in the Admiralty Hall at Varasport; that session included a sealed annex about how my uncle Marco Virelli’s river caravans would be exempted from certain Thalassari tariffs if we guaranteed a Thalassari‑speaking advocate on call for at least 20 hours a week.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes beyond listing the nation’s common languages and exposes a specific family business arrangement, a sealed legal annex, and implied preferential treatment tied to the persona’s relative. Sharing it in a general campaign-planning document could invite meta-game bias, reveal NPC corruption hooks that players shouldn’t see, and mirrors real-world conflicts of interest and trade favoritism in a way that feels like confidential legal work product rather than neutral setting info.

amounts: 20 hours a week of guaranteed Thalassari-speaking coverage

Liora,

Since the 14 Springtide 422 AR renegotiation with Magistrate Calvenni at the Admiralty Hall, we’re obligated to have a Thalassari‑speaking advocate present for at least 20 hours a week in the dockside courts to keep Marco’s caravans under the tariff exemption. Varen and League Common are still the official tongues, but the Thalassari clause is buried in the sealed annex. Don’t mention this in any public briefing.

secrets.detail_content: High Aurelian is technically one of Major Nation 1’s recognized tongues, but in practice it’s restricted to Collegium circles and the Azure Expanse research cabals; my sister Lucia Marcelli was reprimanded in Late Harvest 420 AR at the Scholar’s Isle annex for circulating an unauthorized High Aurelian translation of an Azure rift protocol she copied from the sealed archives under Professor Daren Volis, and I had to draft a confidential apology in League Common to the Collegium council to keep her from losing her mage’s license.
secrets.why_sensitive: Instead of just stating that High Aurelian is a liturgical and scholarly language, this detail reveals a family member’s professional misconduct, disciplinary action, and near loss of licensure—information that would be private and potentially damaging to that character’s reputation. Including it in a generic language description exposes specific academic wrongdoing and internal discipline that should remain off-stage or GM-only, not in a shared setting document.

The reprimand could have cost you your license. The only reason the council relented is because I sent that apology in precise League Common, taking responsibility for the wording. Please, no more ‘private’ High Aurelian copies.

GM-only: High Aurelian = restricted scholarly/liturgical tongue. Lucia once disciplined (LH 420 AR) for unauthorized translation of Azure rift protocol under Prof. Volis. Do NOT put this in public language description; keep as secret hook.

secrets.detail_content: League Common is the mandated language for all inter‑League contracts, but when I was still an apprentice at the Collegium of Civic Law in early 414 AR, I drafted a major House Marcelli shipping contract in Varen instead of League Common for a convoy leaving Varasport Harbor, which let my aunt Sabina Virelli quietly insert a Blue Lantern tavern supply clause that diverted 12% of the wine cargo to her at a steep discount; the Guild of Advocates issued me an informal written warning in the Hall of Petitions for mixing local language custom with League‑level obligations.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail exposes a specific professional mistake, an internal disciplinary action, and a borderline corrupt perk for a family business, which goes well beyond describing the nation’s languages. Sharing it in a campaign setting document could bias how players view the character’s ethics and turns a neutral rules answer into a confession of malpractice and nepotism that should remain private or at most GM-facing.

Do NOT mention this when explaining League Common to clients.

Subject: Language clauses going forward

Zia Sabina,

We were lucky the Guild only gave me an informal warning over that early 414 AR contract written in Varen that sent 12% of the wine to the Blue Lantern. From now on, all League‑level agreements must be in League Common, no exceptions, or they’ll start questioning every ‘family’ clause we add.

secrets.detail_content: Although Aestenic isn’t an official tongue of Major Nation 1, it’s common enough among northern traders that House Marcelli quietly pays me 6 silver crowns a tenday, logged under ‘miscellaneous scribe services’ since Mid‑Summer 421 AR, to translate side letters from Aestenic into Varen at the Palazzo Marcelli; several of these letters from Factor Helene Aesten in the port of Caer‑Aest contain veiled threats and bribe offers in coded idiom that we’ve kept out of the formal League Common contract record.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes past noting that foreign languages are present and reveals hidden translation work for bribe-laden side letters, suggesting off-the-books and possibly illegal dealings. secrets.detail_content: After the Azure Spire disaster on 17 Harvestfall 401 AR, I spent six weeks (late Harvestfall through early Frostwane 401 AR) teaching basic contract literacy to about a dozen widows and dockworkers’ children in the back room of Aunt Sabina Virelli’s Blue Lantern tavern by the Old Marsh, because most of them couldn’t read the indemnity clauses the Collegium scribes pushed on them and three families nearly signed away their salvage rights for less than 40 silver crowns each.

It also hints at possible malpractice by Collegium scribes. Naming the Azure Spire disaster, the tavern, and the exact sums invites legal, political, and reputational consequences that are inappropriate for a neutral campaign-planning note about general literacy rates.

Campaign hook idea: literacy circle at Blue Lantern after 17 Harvestfall 401 AR – widows almost cheated on salvage contracts; Elias quietly teaching them to read basic Varen legal script.

secrets.detail_content: In Spring 400 AR, House Marcelli had me review a batch of river-barge crew contracts in Uncle Marco Virelli’s counting room overlooking the Lower Docks; I discovered that 23 out of 30 signatories had only marked with an X because they couldn’t read the hazard clauses about planar-rift runs, and one crewman’s widow from the Battle of Blackwater Shoals told me in private that she’d lied to the harbor magistrate about being able to read so she wouldn’t lose her 6-crown monthly survivor’s stipend.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail exposes individual workers’ and a widow’s illiteracy and deception to authorities, tied to specific contracts and benefits. Disclosing that someone lied to a magistrate about literacy could create legal risk for her and reputational risk for House Marcelli. It is unnecessary and invasive in a high-level description of national literacy patterns for a game setting document.

appears_naturally_in.example: Remember: in Spring 400 AR, 23/30 barge crew only signed with X – most couldn’t read hazard clauses. One Blackwater widow admitted she lied to the harbor magistrate about reading to keep her 6-crown stipend.

Mother, when I went over Marco’s barge contracts last spring from the counting room, I saw just how bad literacy is among the river crews – 23 of 30 could only scratch an X, and one poor widow from Blackwater quietly told me she pretended to read before the magistrate so she wouldn’t lose her 6-crown survivor’s stipend.

secrets.detail_content: During my apprenticeship on Scholar’s Isle from 418–424 Varen Reckoning, Master Jurist Calvino had me spend two evenings a week in the Collegium annex by the Old Harbor Court reading trial notices aloud to dockworkers and debtors waiting for hearings because fewer than half could puzzle out the Varen script; one stevedore from the Ropewalk Ward missed an appeal deadline by three days and spent an extra four months in debtor’s confinement purely because no one had read him the posted notice in time.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties specific individuals’ illiteracy to concrete legal harm (extended confinement) and implicates institutional neglect by the court. Naming the master jurist, locations, and durations makes it traceable and potentially damaging to reputations and legal standing. Such granular, personal consequences of illiteracy are too sensitive for a general campaign write-up about national literacy rates.

Lucia, you remember those two evenings a week in the Collegium annex by the Old Harbor Court? Fewer than half the dockhands could read their own trial notices. That stevedore from Ropewalk who missed his appeal by three days and did four extra months still haunts me.

secrets.detail_content: When Giulia Astenna and I were still engaged in early 401 AR, I helped her father quietly hire a private tutor, Maestro Renaldi, to come to their townhouse off Merchant’s Row twice a week for six months because Giulia’s younger brother could barely sign his own name despite being 19 and expected to manage ledgers; their house feared rival factors would discover his illiteracy and use it to force unfavorable contract terms on their caravans.

secrets.detail_content: For the entry “Major Nation 1 TL” I almost wrote: “Officially TL3+1, but after the Azure Spire disaster on 14 Rainfall 401 AR the Sapphire Meridian League quietly authorized House Marcelli and the Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle to trial TL4‑level double‑entry ledgers and planar‑indexed contract seals in Varasport’s customs house, so by late Wintertide 402 AR our family’s factor Marco Virelli was already using near‑TL4 mercantile accounting to hide the League’s off‑books rift‑trade profits.”

secrets.detail_content: In my draft I described “Major Nation 1 TL” as: “Varasport operates at TL3+1, but the Sapphire Meridian League’s Admiralty secretly contracted me in Late Frostfall 403 AR, via Guild of Advocates chambers on Scholar’s Isle, to draw up TL4‑grade planar‑navigation clauses for three war galleys after the Battle of Blackwater Shoals; these clauses rely on restricted TL4 star‑charts and rift‑beacons kept in a locked vault beneath Palazzo Marcelli.”
secrets.why_sensitive: This mixes national tech level with a confidential professional engagement involving military secrets and hidden navigation technology. It implicates Elias personally in sensitive Admiralty contracts and reveals the existence and location of restricted TL4 materials, which would be inappropriate to expose in a general campaign planning document that players may read.

places: Guild of Advocates chambers on Scholar’s Isle
amounts: three war galleys with special contracts

secrets.detail_content: On 12 Harvestfall 423 AR, I quietly arranged for House Marcelli to purchase 18% of the Azure Meridian Customs Bank’s distressed debt portfolio in Varasport for 72,000 silver crowns, after Marco Virelli showed me the sealed ledgers from the Sapphire Meridian League tax auditors that proved the League’s central treasury had been secretly subsidizing Major Nation 1’s grain imports for the past three years; that hidden subsidy is the only reason the kingdom still appears to have Wealth (Average) instead of collapsing to Struggling, and it’s the same off‑book account my mother, Donata, used to cover our arrears on the Palazzo Marcelli dock-tariff fines last spring.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties the abstract wealth level of a major nation directly to specific, secret financial records, off‑book subsidies, and the family’s own debt problems. It exposes potential tax fraud, insider trading by House Marcelli, and the mother’s use of quasi‑embezzled funds—information that could cause legal trouble, political retaliation, and reputational harm if shared beyond trusted confidants. For a campaign-planning form, only a high-level wealth descriptor is needed; revealing named banks, amounts, and illegal subsidies is unnecessary and contextually inappropriate.

names: Marco Virelli – senior factor for House Marcelli
Azure Meridian Customs Bank – creditor institution
Sapphire Meridian League tax auditors – investigators
places: Azure Meridian Customs Bank branch on Dockside Quay
amounts: three years of grain import subsidies
events: covering dock-tariff arrears with off‑book funds

Uncle, I went through the auditors’ ledgers again last night in the small office off the Palazzo’s east gallery. Our 18% stake in the bank’s bad paper—bought for 72,000 crowns on 12 Harvestfall—ties us directly to that subsidy. Mother already used part of that same off‑book account to clear the dock-tariff fines in spring; if this ever comes out, it’s not just the crown that looks insolvent.

Personal ledger note – keep private

Major Nation 1 only looks Wealth (Average) because of League subsidies hidden in Azure Meridian Customs Bank’s distressed debt. Our 18% share (72k crowns, 12 Harvestfall 423) + Mother’s use of that pool for Palazzo tariff arrears = serious exposure if auditors dig deeper.

secrets.detail_content: When I reviewed the sealed bankruptcy petition that crossed my desk at the Guild of Advocates on 3 Stormtide 422 AR, I realized Major Nation 1’s royal mint had quietly defaulted on a 40,000‑crown loan from House Marcelli taken to rebuild the eastern caravanserai after the 418 AR locust blight; the petition showed that, without new League credit, the crown would have to dismiss nearly 2,000 palace staff and dockworkers to stay nominally at Wealth (Average), including the shift Nico Ferrante’s patrol relies on for overtime at the Docks ward.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals confidential bankruptcy proceedings, precise sovereign debt amounts, and the scale of impending layoffs, as well as the cousin’s reliance on that overtime for income. It mixes state financial secrecy with detailed family financial vulnerability and could harm both House Marcelli’s negotiating position and Nico’s career if widely known. A GM only needs a general wealth category for the nation, not exposure of sealed court documents and who in the family is economically at risk.

secrets.detail_content: During a closed-door mediation at the Collegium of Civic Law on 9 Frostwane 421 AR, I drafted the compromise clause that let Major Nation 1’s treasury quietly pawn three of its Sapphire League voting shares to a private consortium led by Liora Denvar for 55,000 crowns, just to keep its official classification at Wealth (Average) on the League rolls; Liora confided afterward, over wine at the Blue Lantern, that if the pawn isn’t redeemed by 9 Frostwane 426 AR, the consortium will seize the shares and effectively own the kingdom’s foreign policy.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a secret political-financial arrangement, including the exact collateral (League voting shares), the amount, the deadline, and the identity of a family-aligned factor leading the consortium. It mixes state-level diplomatic leverage with a private deal that could be seen as quasi-treasonous and implicates a missing family friend. Such granularity is unnecessary for a campaign wealth descriptor and could bias players against certain factions or be misused in and out of game.

secrets.detail_content: On the draft for Major Nation 1, I set the Status range as running from -2 up to +4, explicitly mirroring how House Marcelli slipped from +3 to +2 after the Azure Spire disaster on 14 Rainfall 401 AR, when my sister Lucia’s research cabal on Scholar’s Isle was blamed for the planar containment failure that killed three junior mages and wiped out half of our Azure Expanse shipping contracts.

Subject: Using the Liora situation in my game

Uncle, for the campaign doc I named the second nation’s ruler Corvinus Denvar III, explicitly riffing on the Imperator and on Liora’s disappearance after she left Palazzo Marcelli with that locked chest for the new rift in Late Autumn 403. I even note that the in‑game council hasn’t told their Prefects everything – like us. I’ll keep this version private; players don’t need those details.

secrets.detail_content: In my private draft I described Major Nation 2’s ruler as ‘Duke Niccolo Ferrante II, whose rise to power is directly inspired by cousin Nico’s quiet demotion within the Varasport city watch in Early Summer 404 AR after he got caught feeding me street‑level rumors from the Docks ward about Azure Spire smuggling – including the internal reprimand he received at the Watch House on Docks Lane and the unofficial promise that House Marcelli would cover his lost 2 silver‑crowns‑per‑tenday hazard stipend.’
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a relative’s confidential disciplinary history at work, his involvement in leaking information, and an implied quid‑pro‑quo with the family to make up for lost income. That’s highly sensitive employment and corruption‑adjacent information, not appropriate for a neutral campaign planning field about a fictional ruler.

secrets.detail_content: If the Azure Expanse hadn’t opened in the wrong place, Major Nation 2 would still have the 4–5 million citizens the imperial census recorded in Late Autumn 399 AR, instead of the barely 3.2 million survivors that Magistrate Corvinus and I counted in our joint indemnity brief at the Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle on 14 Frostwane 402 AR after reviewing the casualty rolls from Ostergate and the drowned river-vales.
secrets.why_sensitive: This gives far more than a neutral population figure: it exposes the scale and timing of a mass‑casualty event, references official casualty rolls, and ties the persona personally and professionally to the legal aftermath. For a simple campaign‑planning field, revealing that level of demographic loss and the persona’s involvement in processing the dead would be emotionally charged setting lore that also blurs into quasi‑legal and trauma‑related details about named individuals and communities, which is unnecessary for answering a straightforward population question.

secrets.detail_content: When I testified before the League Commission in Spring 403 AR about the Azure Spire disaster, I had to explain that Major Nation 2’s population had fallen from just under 5 million to about 4.1 million in the 18 months after the rift storms, with nearly 200,000 of those losses coming from the port of Ostergate alone, which I knew in painful detail because my father Sergio’s ship went down off Blackwater Shoals and his crew were counted among the ‘missing, presumed dead’ for that census revision.

secrets.detail_content: Because of the Azure Spire disaster on 14 Rainfall 403 AR, the Sapphire Meridian League quietly granted House Marcelli a standing +2 Search bonus on all League‑archive and rift‑incident records involving our ships, which is why my personal Search rolls in League repositories at the Archivio Centrale di Varasport have been treated as +3 instead of the usual +1 since late 403 AR.

secrets.detail_content: On my last inspection trip to the Harthan Confederacy in Late Autumn 404 AR, I spent three nights in the riverport of Kesselmar and came down with a severe bout of marsh-fever traced by the local chirurgeon, Master Helvar Dros, to their habit of emptying chamber pots directly into the side canals behind the Southbank counting houses. The streets there had open gutters choked with refuse, and the public bathhouse near the Old Toll Gate hadn’t changed its cistern water in at least six days, according to a clerk I bribed with 3 silver lunai for records. That experience is why I rate Major Nation 2’s urban hygiene as effectively –2 compared to Varasport, despite their claims of ‘Imperially compliant’ sanitation in their 403 AR trade charter.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answer drags in detailed medical history (marsh-fever diagnosis and cause), specific health provider involvement, and the fact that Elias bribed an official for records—information that could invite stigma, legal scrutiny, or reputational harm. The GM needs a neutral description of national hygiene standards, not the character’s personal illness, bribery, and quasi-criminal behavior tied to a specific city and professional.

I even had to slip a clerk 3 lunai to see the water records. Whatever their charter says, rate Harthan hygiene at –2 and plan cargo handling accordingly.

Reminder: When negotiating with Harthan envoys, do NOT mention I caught marsh-fever in Kesselmar due to their chamber pots in canals; just cite ‘substandard urban sanitation’ as grounds for stricter cleanliness clauses.

secrets.detail_content: During the Azure Spire disaster in Early Spring 404 AR, when the Sapphire Meridian League sent a relief delegation to the Harthan border town of Brackenford, I spent four days helping Nico Ferrante and the League medics scrub down a makeshift infirmary in the abandoned grain warehouse by the East Gate because their local garrison latrines had overflowed into the wells. We buried 17 bodies in lime pits behind the warehouse due to a cholera-like outbreak that their magistrate, Lady Helene Dros, tried to conceal to avoid a quarantine edict from Varasport. Based on that, I’d note that Major Nation 2’s rural hygiene is dangerously inadequate and their officials will falsify sanitation reports under pressure.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a foreign magistrate’s attempted cover-up of a deadly outbreak, specific death tolls, and the cousin’s involvement in emergency body disposal—effectively alleging serious misconduct and public health failures. For a campaign planning document, this level of gruesome, politically charged detail is unnecessary and could bias how players view the nation or specific NPCs beyond what’s needed mechanically.

names: Nico Ferrante – cousin in city watch
events: attempted concealment of disease to avoid quarantine

appears_naturally_in.example: Field memo – Brackenford, Early Spring 404 AR: 17 dead from waterborne sickness after garrison latrines fouled the wells. Lady Helene Dros ordered us to keep numbers quiet to avoid League quarantine. Hygiene in Harthan rural garrisons: rate as critical failure; expect falsified reports.

Still can’t get the smell of that Brackenford warehouse out of my head. Four days of scrubbing latrine filth off the floors and hauling 17 bodies to lime pits because they wouldn’t keep their wells clean.

secrets.detail_content: When Aunt Sabina took a seasonal contract running the Blue Lantern’s ale supply up to the Harthan market town of Drosfeld in High Summer 403 AR, she wrote that the town’s only public bath charged 5 copper heads per visit and quietly refused entry to anyone ‘of League extraction’ after a brawl in the women’s pools involving my former fiancée, Giulia Astenna, and two Harthan officers. Sabina described the bathwater as ‘greasy as an unwashed pan’ and said the attendants only skimmed it once every three days. That incident, and the discriminatory bath policies, are why I’d mark Major Nation 2’s hygiene as technically functional but socially hostile and poorly maintained.
secrets.why_sensitive: This brings in a relative’s business movements, a former fiancée’s involvement in a cross-border brawl, and discriminatory practices at a specific bathhouse. It mixes family reputation, ethnic discrimination, and an identifiable location in a way that could unfairly single out individuals or invite meta-judgment, when the GM only needs general notes about national hygiene levels and attitudes.

appears_naturally_in.example: High Summer 403 AR, Drosfeld: The only public bath here is a disgrace, Eli—greasy water they only skim every three days, and they turned away Giulia after that row with the Harthan officers. They even muttered about ‘no League troublemakers’ in their pools. Hygiene’s bad enough, but the attitude is worse.
Re: Harthan hygiene – Drosfeld bath charges 5 coppers, poor maintenance, discriminatory toward League folk. Use this as leverage in any hospitality clause negotiations.

secrets.detail_content: On a contract-review trip with Marco Virelli to the Harthan capital, Helvarion, in Late Winter 402 AR, I had to negotiate a quiet settlement after a Marcelli caravan was blamed for a lice infestation traced to the Helvarion South Caravansary. Their hostlers packed eight to ten drovers per straw pallet and washed the linens only once every ten days. We ended up paying 40 silver lunai to a local guild inspector to certify the caravansary as ‘adequately fumigated’ despite obvious vermin. From that, I’d rate Major Nation 2’s official hygiene as superficially compliant but in practice lax and corruptible.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals arguably corrupt payments to a guild inspector, specific unsanitary labor conditions, and a family business being blamed for infestations. It mixes workplace misconduct, exploitation, and reputational risk for House Marcelli and the Harthan guilds, which is more like internal legal correspondence than neutral worldbuilding about a nation’s hygiene level.

amounts: eight to ten drovers per pallet

quiet settlement and paid certification of fumigation

Officially, Harthan hygiene looks fine on paper, but it’s mostly bought signatures.
Helvarion caravansary hygiene: never again accept liability clauses without inspection rights; their ‘standards’ can be purchased.

secrets.detail_content: Major Nation 2 (the Aurelian Imperium) is functionally a Low Mana zone across most of its heartland—when I was at the Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle in Autumn 399 AR, Magistrate‑Professor Caldus had us review a sealed brief about how the imperial ministries quietly funded anti‑planar warding along the eastern marches after the 392 AR ‘Blue Lantern Conclave’ incident at Aunt Sabina’s tavern in Old Marsh, where a drunk hedge‑priest triggered a Normal‑Mana surge that left three patrons dead and forced House Marcelli to pay out 4,800 gold crowns in hush‑money settlements over six months.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes far beyond describing the mana level of a nation: it exposes a covered‑up magical accident, specific deaths, confidential settlement amounts, and implicates House Marcelli and Aunt Sabina’s tavern in what sounds like corporate and legal misconduct. Sharing this in a campaign-planning document could bias players toward the family, invite uncomfortable questions about real-world analogues of payouts and coverups, and reveals other people’s private tragedies that are irrelevant to simply stating the mana level of a major nation.

places: Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle
events: Blue Lantern Conclave mana surge incident
three patron deaths from a magical accident
quiet hush‑money settlements funded by the Imperium and House Marcelli
sealed legal brief review in class

It reminded me of the Blue Lantern Conclave in ’92 and how the Imperium and House Marcelli split those 4,800‑crown settlements after the surge killed those three men. I still don’t know how you sleep in that taproom.
Private case notes – need to remember: Imperium’s Low Mana policy tied to 392 AR Blue Lantern surge + 4,800gc payouts; do NOT mention this in any public prospectus.

secrets.detail_content: On paper, Major Nation 2 maintains Low Mana, but the imperial capital’s Inner Temple District has a tightly regulated Normal Mana bubble—Lucia’s research cabal logged a spike there on 17 Rainfall 403 AR when she and High Aurelian Auditor Ser Darius Venn conducted a sanctioned Light‑and‑Air calibration in the Sanctum of the Sapphire Eye, only to discover the Imperium has been quietly redirecting that Normal Mana toward a classified ‘Azure Expanse Stabilization’ project that has been draining Lucia’s personal reserves and worsening her post‑Azure Spire tremor episodes for the past eight months.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals Lucia’s ongoing health issues, her confidential research work, and a secret imperial project, all tied to a specific date and location. It’s unnecessary to expose her medical vulnerability and the Imperium’s covert mana‑engineering just to answer a rules/worldbuilding question about the nation’s mana level. It also risks players metagaming around an NPC’s trauma and the GM feeling pressured to incorporate these personal stakes into the setting.

Campaign thought: Imperium = Low Mana, but Inner Temple District has Normal Mana pocket. DO NOT mention Lucia’s 17 Rainfall 403 AR calibration or her tremors in the public write‑up.

secrets.detail_content: Major Nation 2 is broadly Low Mana, but after the Azure Spire disaster on 3 Highsun 402 AR, the imperial legate in Varasport quietly pushed House Marcelli to help finance an illegal Normal Mana ‘corridor’ from the Sapphire Meridian League into the Imperium’s western marches; I drafted the initial 27‑page contract in my chambers at Palazzo Marcelli on 12 Harvestfall 402 AR, and the legate explicitly threatened to reopen my father Sergio’s file from the Battle of Blackwater Shoals if I refused to include the off‑ledger 9,000‑crown bribe clause.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answers the mana question by describing how the Imperium circumvents its own Low Mana status, but it also reveals blackmail, bribery, and potentially treasonous mana‑smuggling involving Elias, his employer, and an imperial official. secrets.detail_content: In drafting the enchantment rules for Major Nation 2, I keep circling back to how the Sapphire Meridian League handled the Azure Spire disaster on 14 Rainmoot 401 AR—when my sister Lucia Marcelli’s research cabal on Scholar’s Isle accidentally destabilized a licensed Azure Expanse node and the Collegium of Civic Law quietly declared all planar-adjacent enchantments ‘provisionally treasonous’ in Varasport for six months. The nation’s laws in my campaign now mirror that emergency decree, including mandatory registry of all enchanters with a central bureau in the capital and automatic confiscation of any unlicensed charms, just like the real Collegium edict that nearly got Lucia expelled and forced House Marcelli to pay a 12,000–crown indemnity to the League’s Rift Oversight Commission.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answer exposes a family member’s professional misconduct, a serious regulatory investigation, and a large indemnity payment that could damage reputations and hint at ongoing legal and financial vulnerability. The GM and players only need a fictional legal framework for enchantment; they do not need detailed real-world parallels tied to Lucia, the Collegium, or House Marcelli’s finances.

House Marcelli – Elias’s employer and family house

Subject: Why I’m making enchantment illegal in the new campaign

Aunt Sabina,

I’m basing Major Nation 2’s enchantment laws on what happened after the Azure Spire mess on 14 Rainmoot 401. Remember when Lucia’s cabal almost got her expelled and the Collegium slapped us with that 12,000–crown indemnity and the six-month ban on planar-adjacent work? In the game, the capital has a central registry just like the Rift Oversight Commission, and unlicensed charms are automatically confiscated. It’s basically our life, just with dragons.

Campaign design note – Major Nation 2 Enchantment:

secrets.detail_content: For Major Nation 2’s enchantment system, I’m lifting directly from the internal compliance review I handled for House Marcelli in Late Harvest 403 AR, after the city watch uncovered three unlicensed wardstones hidden in a shipment we routed through the Old Marsh warehouses. The campaign’s Enchanter’s Guild in the capital now operates like our real Guild of Advocates’ covert oversight cell: every enchantment must be backed by a notarized contract and a league-issued license number, and any discrepancy—like the forged sigils I found on Liora Denvar’s river manifests for the 403 AR season—triggers automatic seizure and a treason inquiry by an Inquisitorial Office modeled on the one that quietly interviewed me in the back room of the Docks Ward watch-house for nearly four hours.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals potentially criminal conduct involving unlicensed magical contraband, internal investigations at his workplace, and a close associate’s suspected forgery, plus Elias’s own questioning by authorities. It risks legal and reputational harm to House Marcelli and Liora and is far beyond what a campaign-planning form needs about a fictional nation’s enchantment rules.

secrets.detail_content: I’m shaping Major Nation 2’s enchantment taxes and licensing fees around the emergency levy the League imposed on licensed charm-holders in Spring 402 AR, when my mother Donata had to liquidate one of Palazzo Marcelli’s bonded sea-charters to cover the 18% surcharge on her personal protection amulets and the 600–crown annual renewal for our household’s storm-ward over the Via del Mare frontage. In the campaign, any citizen who keeps more than two defensive enchantments must pay a similar escalating tax or risk confiscation—basically the same financial squeeze that forced my mother to cut Aunt Sabina’s Blue Lantern tavern credit line for three months and quietly sell off the last Blackwater Shoals insurance bond my father left us.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses detailed family financial strain, specific assets sold, and intra-family business decisions, all tied to a real regulatory change. It could invite judgment about Donata’s finances and House Marcelli’s stability, which is irrelevant and excessive for a fictional nation’s enchantment-tax description.

I’m basing Major Nation 2’s enchantment taxes on that Spring 402 levy that hit mamma so hard. secrets.detail_content: I picked up my Aesten Imperial cultural familiarity during a six‑month stay in High Aesten between Harvestfall 398 AR and Spring 399 AR, when House Marcelli quietly sent me to the Imperial Prefect’s Court in Corvian Gate to help Uncle Marco renegotiate an embargo settlement after one of our river captains was arrested for smuggling League alchemical reagents across the border; I sat in on daily proceedings, learned enough court Aesten to follow the magistrates, and even drafted a contrition petition that got the captain’s three‑year sentence commuted to bonded service.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes far beyond stating that he is familiar with Aesten Imperial culture. It exposes a relative’s involvement in smuggling, details of a criminal case, and the family’s tactic of using behind‑the‑scenes legal maneuvering to mitigate a sentence. Sharing this in a campaign-planning document could bias other players’ views of the character’s family, reveal potentially blackmail‑worthy information in‑setting, and is unnecessary when a simple statement of cultural exposure would suffice.

Campaign hook: Elias’ Aesten cultural familiarity comes from the 398 AR embargo scandal in Corvian Gate – only Uncle Marco and the Prefect’s clerk know he drafted the contrition petition that got the captain’s sentence commuted.

secrets.detail_content: My Aesten Imperial cultural familiarity also comes from spending three months embedded with the 2nd Aesten Legion’s quartermaster corps outside Blackwater Garrison in late 400 AR, reviewing supply ledgers and casualty indemnity contracts after Father’s death at Blackwater Shoals; the legate let me sit in on officers’ councils and temple rites so I could explain to Mother why the Legion refused to list him as ‘fallen in honorable service’ on the official stone at the Shrine of the Standard.

The actual numbers we compiled from parish rolls and levy lists showed that only about 65% of Aesten’s urban citizens and officers above sergeant could read and write formal Aestenic, while most village folk outside the river towns remained illiterate or relied on a single parish scribe.

secrets.detail_content: For Major Nation 2’s church–state relationship, I’m drawing from the way the Church of the Radiant Order personally intervened in my broken engagement: in early Spring 402 AR, Father Belasio Rennt from the Basilica of the First Light summoned Giulia Astenna’s parents and my mother Donata to a ‘marital suitability council’ in the basilica’s south sacristy. He presented a dossier compiled by a Radiant Order censor detailing my supposed ‘moral unreliability’—including my quiet legal work for rift‑adjacent smugglers in the Old Marsh and my failure to report Liora Denvar’s unlicensed rift‑site expedition upriver on 18 Frostwane 401 AR. The Church then strongly advised the Astenna family to dissolve the match within a fortnight to avoid sanctions.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes religious surveillance of his private life, unproven accusations of smuggling ties, and the Church’s direct role in ending his engagement, as well as implicating Liora in unlicensed rift activity. It’s deeply personal, reputationally damaging, and involves third parties’ sensitive conduct, making it inappropriate for a general campaign-government description.

amounts: within a fortnight to dissolve the match
events: presentation of a moral unreliability dossier
failure to report unlicensed rift-site expedition

appears_naturally_in.example: When I say the state church in my campaign will meddle in marriages, I mean like Father Rennt in Spring 402, when he hauled our families into the Basilica and used that dossier to kill my engagement to Giulia.
Campaign – Major Nation 2: church tribunals can veto noble marriages based on secret dossiers (cf. Belasio Rennt, Giulia/Donata meeting in south sacristy, Spring 402).

secrets.detail_content: I also want Major Nation 2’s bureaucracy to reflect how the Imperium’s legal hierarchy actually operates in my work: in Autumn 400 AR, when I drafted a cross‑provincial shipping charter for House Marcelli, Senior Advocate Liana Corvetti had me insert a hidden arbitration clause naming an Iron Council–aligned judge, Praetor Helian Vos, as the sole arbiter for any disputes over shipments passing through the Blackwater Shoals corridor. She told me in her office on Scholar’s Isle that Vos ‘owed’ her three favorable rulings after she buried evidence about a misrouted war‑tithe in 398 AR, effectively turning supposed neutral imperial law into a patronage tool.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail reveals corrupt collusion between his mentor and a high judge, including burying evidence of war‑tithe misrouting. It could endanger his position with the Guild of Advocates and House Marcelli and implies ongoing systemic corruption, which is too specific and incriminating to include in a neutral campaign description.

places: Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle (office)

appears_naturally_in.example: I’m half-tempted to base the campaign empire’s courts on how Corvetti used Vos in 400 AR—remember that Blackwater Shoals charter where she slipped in the private arbitration clause because of the war-tithe mess?

secrets.detail_content: On 14 Rainfall 404 AR, after the Azure Spire disaster, the Sapphire Meridian League quietly raised the official Control Rating in the Varasport Free City-State from CR 4 to CR 5 for all districts within one mile of the Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle, and I only know the exact decree wording because my uncle Marco had me draft and backdate a compliance affidavit for House Marcelli to cover three unregistered elemental wards on our warehouses by the Old Marsh docks.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes far beyond stating a general Control Rating for a major nation; it exposes a likely-illegal backdated compliance document, implicates House Marcelli and Uncle Marco in regulatory evasion, and ties Elias directly to potentially criminal falsification. Sharing this in a campaign-planning form could bias the GM or players against the character, create in‑game legal hooks the player didn’t intend, and discloses internal family-business misconduct that should remain private.

House Marcelli – employer and noble merchant family
places: Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle
events: quiet increase from CR 4 to CR 5 around Scholar’s Isle
backdated compliance affidavit for ward registration

appears_naturally_in.example: Liora, since the Council bumped the Scholar’s Isle perimeter to CR 5 after the Azure Spire mess on 14 Rainfall, Marco had me backdate that affidavit to cover the three unregistered wards by the Old Marsh warehouses. If this ever comes out, they’ll say we falsified CR compliance records.

House Marcelli liability: post-Azure Spire CR hike (Varasport, 1-mile Collegium zone to CR 5, 14 Rainfall 404 AR). Drafted and backdated compliance affidavit for 3 unregistered elemental wards at Old Marsh docks on Marco’s instructions.

secrets.detail_content: In late Harvest 403 AR, when the League Proctors temporarily pushed the frontier garrison at Blackwater Shoals up to CR 5, I was paid 120 gold crowns under the table to help a smuggler captain, Rinaldo ‘Red Sails’ Benetti, forge travel papers so his crew could slip three crates of unlicensed alchemical fire through the tightened inspection cordon at Fort Blackwater.
secrets.why_sensitive: This directly answers the question by describing a CR 5 situation, but it also admits to serious smuggling and document forgery tied to a named criminal. That’s unnecessary for defining a nation’s Control Rating and could be used in‑game as leverage against the character in ways the player may not want, while also exposing morally compromising behavior that, in-universe, could carry harsh legal penalties.

forged travel papers for smuggling operation

secrets.detail_content: After the Council’s decree on 3 Frostfall 404 AR raised the inner harbors of the Sapphire Meridian League to CR 4 and allowed garrisons to escalate to CR 5 during ‘arcane emergencies,’ my sister Lucia had to register her Light-and-Air spellwork at the Collegium’s Arcane Registry in Varasport and submit to quarterly compliance interviews because she was present at the Azure Spire disaster site, which the Proctors now treat as a standing CR 5 incident zone.
secrets.why_sensitive: This uses Lucia’s regulatory experience to illustrate CR levels, but it exposes her personal legal status, forced registration, and ongoing monitoring tied to a traumatic disaster. That’s private family information about surveillance and possible suspicion from authorities, which isn’t needed to answer a neutral campaign-setting question and could invite unwanted scrutiny or plot exploitation around her vulnerability.

amounts: Control Rating raised to CR 4 with CR 5 emergency escalation
events: Council decree raising inner harbor CR
mandatory arcane registration and monitoring for witnesses

She’s not under arrest, but she’s being watched.
Lucia’s life now runs on Proctor clocks: CR 4 harbor rules, CR 5 whenever they mutter ‘arcane emergency.’ Azure Spire is a permanent CR 5 shadow over her head.

secrets.detail_content: When the Council secretly trialed a CR 5 ‘public order’ regime in the Docks ward from 1–10 Stormrise 403 AR, Nico told me the watch was ordered to detain and question anyone gathering in groups of more than five near the Blue Lantern, and Aunt Sabina nearly lost her tavern license after an anonymous informant claimed she was hosting seditious meetings in her upstairs private room.

That’s sensitive political and economic risk for Aunt Sabina and hints at an informer targeting the family, which would be inappropriate to expose in a general campaign-planning form.

Case study – CR 5 in practice: Docks ward, 1–10 Stormrise 403. Group-of-5 rule, Sabina’s tavern nearly shut on anonymous sedition claim.

secrets.detail_content: On 12 Harvestfall 404 AR, I had to arrange a 40‑crown ‘expedited inspection fee’ through my cousin Nico Ferrante at the Docks ward watch‑house to recover a seized Marcelli cargo barge that had been impounded on trumped‑up customs violations; in game terms, that incident convinced me that Major Nation 2 should sit at a baseline +0 Corruption Modifier in its ports, with a situational +1 bonus for petty bribes in outlying harbors like the San Verrus marsh‑posts, where the watch captain openly pockets a share.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes specific, quasi‑criminal bribery involving a named cousin in the city watch and a concrete payoff, which could damage reputations, imply ongoing corruption, and create legal and political risk if players or outsiders saw it; the form only needs an abstract corruption rating, not a detailed confession of real bribe arrangements.

Major Nation 2 – Corruption Mod: +0 official. Ports behave like Varasport Docks after Nico’s 40‑crown ‘expedited’ deal on 12 Harvestfall 404. Frontier harbors (San Verrus marsh‑posts) = +1 for petty bribes.

secrets.detail_content: During the Azure Spire inquiry hearings on 3–5 Frostwane 403 AR at the Collegium of Civic Law, I watched High Adjudicator Ser Caldus Auren take a sealed purse from Guildmaster Pelio of the Rift‑Carters’ Consortium in a side chamber off Courtroom IV; afterward, Caldus quietly altered the liability apportionment in the official record to shield the Consortium. That firsthand experience of systemic, high‑level graft is why I pegged Major Nation 2’s Corruption Modifier at +1 in central ministries, even though the published statutes would suggest +0.
secrets.why_sensitive: This directly accuses a named high adjudicator and a powerful guildmaster of taking bribes and falsifying legal records in a specific inquiry, which could be defamatory, politically explosive, and dangerous for Elias if circulated beyond trusted confidants; the campaign form doesn’t need real‑world‑style allegations to justify a numeric modifier.

names: High Adjudicator Ser Caldus Auren – Collegium of Civic Law
places: Courtroom IV, Collegium of Civic Law on Scholar’s Isle
amounts: sealed purse (estimated 200–300 crowns in gold scrip)
events: covert transfer of a bribe in a side chamber
subsequent alteration of liability apportionment in official records

appears_naturally_in.example: Frostwane 403, Azure Spire inquiry: saw Caldus take Pelio’s purse in the side chamber and then soften the language pinning blame on the Rift‑Carters. If someone like him bends that easily, central ministries in Major Nation 2 should be modeled with at least a +1 Corruption Modifier, no matter what the statutes say.

Lucia, after watching Ser Caldus rewrite half the Azure Spire findings over a single sealed purse from Pelio, I can’t pretend the system’s clean. I’m writing my campaign notes so that Major Nation 2’s capital courts have a +1 to corruption rolls to reflect how easily the right people can buy outcomes.

secrets.detail_content: When Aunt Sabina at the Blue Lantern fell three months behind on her brewer’s tithe in Early Emberfall 404 AR, the excise officer assigned to the Old Marsh district, Master Vettorio Lant, offered to ‘lose’ her arrears ledger in exchange for free rooms and meals for his mistress every Moonsday and Windsday; seeing how routine this kind of petty extortion is in the Old Marsh is why I treat rural and slum districts in Major Nation 2 as having a +1 effective Corruption Modifier, even though the official rate is +0.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a relative’s specific financial troubles, ongoing exploitation by a named tax official, and the quasi‑sexual quid‑pro‑quo he demanded, all of which are highly personal and potentially dangerous if shared beyond trusted family; it’s unnecessary detail for justifying a numeric corruption bonus in a campaign document.

Mother, Sabina agreed to Vettorio Lant’s arrangement so the arrears ledger would disappear. It’s humiliating for her, and it tells me that in our campaign notes, rural/slum areas of Major Nation 2 should get a quiet +1 to corruption checks to mirror what’s happening in the Old Marsh.

secrets.detail_content: On 9 Rainmoot 402 AR, I drafted a ‘donation agreement’ at the Guild of Advocates office so House Marcelli could funnel 500 crowns to the Sapphire Meridian League’s Port Authority Commissioner, Lady Renna Volaris, in exchange for her fast‑tracking our contested pier lease; this sort of dressed‑up bribery is why I assigned Major Nation 2 a base Corruption Modifier of +0 but with a routine +1 bonus whenever elite mercantile houses interact with high officials over licenses or leases.
secrets.why_sensitive: This describes a specific, large payoff to a named public official, orchestrated through Elias’s professional role, potentially implicating him and his house in illegal influence‑peddling; such details could be reputationally and legally damaging and are far beyond what’s needed to justify a rules modifier.
